text: If you want to get better at cinematography, you've got to practice all the time. But most of us don't have easy access to a big crew or actors or a lot of fancy gear. So, what do we do? Well, why not practice filming yourself? In this video, we're going to talk about how to find inspiration and how to find interesting locations, which gear to use, whether you're a beginner or you're more advanced. Then, I'll take you through some specific techniques like shooting with all manual cine lenses. In this case, I'll be using the DZO film Vespid 2 kit. I'll show you some special techniques like filming wide low angle shots, defocusing yourself in the foreground, and shooting B-roll with longer lenses. Then, we're going to dive into some minimalist lighting techniques using the tiny Gutox ML100R lights to add just the right touch to the natural light in the scene. We'll do a few quick indoor shots. Then, we'll simulate daylight outdoors, and then we'll use the lights to create a neon glow at night. And finally, I'll show a few experimental techniques for using AI to replace the lights with practical sources for some interesting results. And at least you need one lens. And I recommend a prime lens because they generally have more character in the image and they have wider apertures. I'll be using the DZO Film Invested 2 lenses in this video, but most primes will work just fine for your practice. It's more important to pay attention to what you put in front of the lens. If you're only going to use one lens, I recommend starting with a 35mm f1.8. If you're going to be shooting in the daytime, you do need a variable ND filter so you can keep your shutter locked at 1/50th of a second if you're shooting 24p or basically double whatever your shutter speed would be. It's called 180° shutter. Then, if you're filming yourself, you will need some kind of a tripod. I have several travel tripods and mini tripods I like to use. I shot this on the Vespit 2 18 mm lens because for this shot, I wanted to show the size of that massive building behind me. So, to make it look huge, I needed a wide-angle lens. Now, we'll go step by step into how I set up and filmed this shot. First, I put the camera on my mini tripod at a low angle. Then, I tilted up the lens about 45°. Then, I walked to my position in the shot and I extended another mini tripod and I set it down there. I used that mini tripod as a standin to set my focus because these lenses are all manual, so I couldn't just rely on autofocus to keep me sharp. Then I lowered that mini tripod, but I left it in place to use as a mark so I knew where to stand in the frame. So then I could just walk up to the tripod and be in perfect focus and framing in my shot. But there was still something missing in this composition. The sky was pretty empty, so I added an airplane. I just grabbed some random image of a plane from online and then I removed the background with one of those free background removing websites. Then I dropped that plane image onto my timeline as a separate layer and I added some gauian blur to defocus it a bit to match the background. And I adjusted its position to make it fly across the frame. Now the shot really takes advantage of the depth of the location. There's action happening in the foreground all the way to the deep distance. Another reason to shoot low and wide is if you have an interesting sign or something above you in the background and you want to feature it and show how big it is. the way to the deep distance. Another reason to shoot low and wide is if you have an interesting sign or something above you in the background and you want to feature it and show how big it is. I set up this shot the same way as the last one, using my selfie stick tripod as a stand-in to set my focus and my framing. Then I pushed down the selfie stick and used it as a mark so I knew where to stand in the frame. This time I put myself in the lower half of the frame so that I wouldn't cover up the sign behind me. Putting myself in the frame below the sign gave a sense of scale. Because there's a person in the frame, you can feel how large the sign is. So, whenever you're in a location that has something interesting overhead, you can try doing this type of low and wide shot to incorporate yourself into a composition with a lot of depth. When filming yourself, it of course feels natural to always put yourself in perfect focus, but since we're shooting with manual lenses, why not get creative with it? One technique I like to do is focusing on the background instead of the subject in the foreground. Not only does this create an interesting composition, it also means you don't have to worry about your subject being out of focus because they're supposed to be out of focus anyway. This tends to work better with telephoto lenses rather than wide lenses because with the telephoto lens, you can defocus your subject more. For this shot, I used the silhouette of this hallway as a framing device. Then I positioned myself in the empty part of the frame where there are no islands over the water and I walked straight toward the camera staying in the same area of the composition. For this shot, I set up my tripod facing the water and waited for a boat. Then once I saw a boat, I ran to my mark and then I continued walking across the frame as the boat moved toward me. This opposing motion makes the shot more interesting because you have a complimentary movement on either side of the frame. Here's another one I did just a few minutes later. This time, all the boats crossing the frame created that opposing motion, and I put myself in the middle as sort of an audience surrogate. In other words, the audience can imagine themselves as me observing all these boats and being in this scene. So, the next time you find an interesting background for your shot, consider putting yourself out of focus in the foreground. Next, we're going to talk about filming B-roll moments using a telephoto lens. A longer lens lets you pick out smaller moments from the big picture, so you can find unique compositions that feel more subjective and more personal. And it also helps you

out of focus in the foreground. Next, we're going to talk about filming B-roll moments using a telephoto lens. A longer lens lets you pick out smaller moments from the big picture, so you can find unique compositions that feel more subjective and more personal. And it also helps you get more shots in a shorter period of time because instead of capturing just a few big wide shots, you can get a lot of detail shots instead. When I'm composing these B-roll shots, I like to look for contrasting elements like the hard angles of this building versus the soft texture of the clouds or the warm colors of the building versus the cooler tones of the other buildings in the distance. Here's another one. Warm colored building, cool colored sky. This shot combines layers of buildings to create an abstract composition. Here, I manually racked focus from the dolphins in the foreground to the skyline in the background. This added some motion to an otherwise static frame. As sunset became dusk, I cooled down my white balance to 4100K to let the image go deep blue. And I just caught random moments as people enjoyed the view. It certainly helps having a beautiful city like Hong Kong as your environment, but you can find great B-roll shots in any location if you just look carefully and you find the right light. This part of the video is proudly sponsored by DZOILM. I asked DZofilm if I could use their Vespit 2 prime lenses because this is a set of relatively compact cine primes that deliver a really sharp and vibrant look which makes them great as an all-purpose kit of lenses for filmmakers shooting solo or shooting with small crews and especially for people shooting on location. These are T1.9 lenses which is the same as f1.8 and they weigh about 800 g each and they have a consistent 77 mm filter thread so you can use the same filters across the whole set. They have a very large image circle covering full-frame Vista Vision sensors and even some medium format cameras. Optically, these lenses are designed to minimize chromatic aberration, lens breathing, and flare. So, you get a tac sharp image from edge to edge at all apertures. So, your shot looks clean but not clinical. They still do have a bit of character in the bokeh. The focus throw is about 270 to 300° depending on the lens, so they will feel familiar if you've ever pulled focus with cine lenses before. One high-end feature is that they support cook eye technology, which captures lens metadata like focus and aperture automatically. Now, this isn't a feature that I personally need, but it is a great timesaver for people who are doing VFX heavy work. But since we're solo shooting here, we're going to do things a much simpler way. Here, I'll be using the Godox ML100R lights, which are tiny, portable 100 W RGB lights that have some amazingly creative accessories. >> >> For this first shot, all I needed was a pop of light to pull me out of the background of the living room because the spot where I wanted to stand was in the shadows. On the ML100R, I put my reflector first. Then on that, I put this collapsible rubber parallel beam adapter, which basically turns it into a spotlight with a 5° spread. I powered it by plugging it into the wall via USBC on my 100 W wall charger. Then I set the white balance of the light to 4100K to match the color of the light coming from the windows. I angled the light down about 45° and I raised it up on my light stand. This is an Ulanzi AT1 tripod and I put a Shinewe carbon fiber rod on top of it to get extra height. So, with that ML100R positioned just out of frame, I was able to get a spot of hard light right where I needed it. So, here was my shot with the light off. And now, here's the shot with the light turned on. Since the beam of light was so narrow, I didn't need to use any additional flags or accessories to control spill. and I was able to walk from the artificial light back into the natural light seamlessly. Next, I wanted to do a shot on the beautiful balcony of this house overlooking the living room because I had that afternoon light filling up the whole background. I just didn't have a spot of light exactly where I wanted to stand in the frame. There's one more little lighting trick I've been playing around with. We're placing the light in post with something else using AI. So, for this shot, I set up the light with the inflatable airsoft tube on the table behind me. I wanted the light to be in frame to simulate a practical source. So, I exported a still frame and I brought it into Google Gemini and I prompted to replace it with a desk lamp and some scattered newspapers like a film noir movie. I brought that still image back into Resolve and I added it as a layer on top of my original footage. Then I did a rough mask in the color tab, added an alpha output, and basically created a split screen where the AI generated image just covers the light and the table. So, here's the final result. So, I brought it into Resolve and dropped it on top of my original footage as another layer. Then, I made a quick mask in the color tab so the AI generated video only covered the left part of the frame. Then, I used the cinematic haze effect on top to create some ambient smoke in the room. On top of that, I added an adjustment layer with some camera shake. And here's the final result. It may look chaotic, but this was 100% safe to shoot.
